title: What Feeling Is Asking to Be Heard Tonight
subtitle: A journal practice for processing emotional residue before sleep
excerpt: A set of journal prompts for Day 6 of the sleep arc. The writing gives the day's emotional residue a place to go — naming the feeling, writing where it sits, and deciding what it needs before the night.
sourceNotes: Theme from Day 6 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795). Prompt structure original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: When you arrive at the end of the day with feelings that haven't had space to finish moving through — use this before sleep, not after lying down.

content.text: Journal prompts for Day 6 of the sleep arc. Use them sitting up — not in bed. Write without editing, five minutes against a timer if you want a container. The goal is to name what's there so it doesn't need to keep the night.
New to journal prompts as a practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The practice
Warm-up
What is the overall feeling of this day?
One word or one sentence. Don't analyse it — just name it.
What is asking to be heard before I sleep tonight?
Write whatever comes — the conversation that went oddly, the thing you didn't say, the small disappointment. Let it land on the page.
Closer in
Where does this feeling sit in my body right now?
Name the location specifically. Chest, jaw, shoulders, stomach. Write what you notice.
Is any of this mine, and is any of it picked up from someone else?
Write both. Name whose energy is whose, as best you can tell.
What does this feeling need before I put it down for the night?
To be named, to be written, to be acknowledged — or something else. Write what it needs, even if you can't fully give it right now.
Where this practice comes from
Built from the Day 6 theme of
SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive
(Rebecca J Page, 2025): easing emotional overload at bedtime. Prompt structure original to homemade.education.
